name: Debug Node.js with Chrome DevTools
description: Attach Chrome DevTools debugger to a Node.js process for breakpoints, stepping, profiling, and live memory inspection.
trendingReason: DevTools integration is the fastest way to debug complex async flows, replacing console.log-driven debugging workflows.

steps.title: Start Node.js with the inspect flag
steps.description: Launch your application with the --inspect flag to start the V8 debugger listening on port 9229.
steps.tip: Use --inspect-brk to pause execution on the very first line, useful for debugging startup issues.

steps.title: Open Chrome DevTools for Node
steps.description: Navigate to the dedicated Node.js debugging page in Chrome to connect to the running process.
steps.tip: Click 'Open dedicated DevTools for Node' for a persistent connection that auto-reconnects on restart.

steps.title: Set breakpoints in source code
steps.description: Add debugger statements in your code or set breakpoints directly in the DevTools Sources panel.
steps.command:
// Add this line where you want to pause:
debugger;

steps.title: Debug with VS Code instead of Chrome
steps.description: Use VS Code's built-in debugger by attaching to the inspect port for an integrated experience.
steps.tip: Set restart: true so the debugger reconnects automatically when you restart the Node process.

steps.title: Debug a running process by PID
steps.description: Attach the debugger to an already-running Node.js process without restarting it.
steps.warning: This sends SIGUSR1 which enables the debugger. On Windows, use: node -e "process._debugProcess(PID)" instead.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Node.js Debug Setup Script
# Configures debugging for development

APP_FILE="${1:-app.js}"
DEBUG_PORT="${2:-9229}"

echo "==> Checking for Node.js..."
node --version || { echo "Node.js not found. Install it first."; exit 1; }

echo "==> Creating VS Code debug configuration..."
mkdir -p .vscode
cat > .vscode/launch.json <<EOF
{
  "version": "0.2.0",
  "configurations": [
    {
      "type": "node",
      "request": "launch",
      "name": "Debug App",
      "program": "${workspaceFolder}/${APP_FILE}",
      "console": "integratedTerminal"
    },
    {
      "type": "node",
      "request": "attach",
      "name": "Attach to Running",
      "port": ${DEBUG_PORT},
      "restart": true
    }
  ]
}
EOF

echo "==> Starting Node.js with debugger..."
echo "    Open chrome://inspect or attach VS Code debugger"
echo ""
node --inspect=${DEBUG_PORT} "${APP_FILE}"

faq.question: Can I debug TypeScript directly with --inspect?
faq.answer: Yes. Use ts-node with --inspect (node --inspect -r ts-node/register app.ts) or compile with source maps enabled. DevTools and VS Code will map back to your TypeScript source files.

faq.question: How do I debug a specific test file?
faq.answer: Run: node --inspect-brk node_modules/.bin/jest --testPathPattern=mytest.test.js --runInBand. The --runInBand flag is important to run tests in the same process as the debugger.

faq.question: Why does my breakpoint not hit?
faq.answer: Common causes: the code path is not executed, source maps are missing or misconfigured, or the file was loaded before the debugger attached. Use --inspect-brk to ensure the debugger is connected before any code runs.
